summary:
Bile duct sludge differs from stones by lacking structural integrity and causing no direct mechanical obstruction; instead, it acts primarily as a precursory state prone to evolving into actual stones due to bile stasis. This distinction affects management decisions since sludge typically doesn't require immediate treatment but carries an increased long-term risk of developing symptomatic gallstones.  

(Note: Only include content directly related to the comparison between sludge and stones.)

Okay, I need to summarize only what Agent1 said about whether bile duct sludge has different clinical significance than bile duct stones — focusing strictly on their differences and implications.

The core point made was that sludge isn’t solid enough to block ducts unlike stones, so it won’t create acute symptoms like jaundice or pancreatitis immediately. Instead, it’s more likely just a transient condition that may eventually turn into real stones if there's stagnant bile flow.

I'll keep this exactly within those boundaries without adding external facts or interpretations.
